responses.text: The target is a March 1, 2019 Intelligencer (New York Magazine) column by Madison Malone Kircher arguing that the so-called Momo Challenge is not real and that adults sharing the rumor are legitimizing a hoax.

The piece is opinion-leaning technology commentary on Intelligencer, which is New York Magazine's politics, business, and tech vertical, so it should be read as analysis rather than original investigative reporting.

The article's central claim—that there is no verified evidence the Momo Challenge has actually caused children to self-harm and that the panic was largely a media-driven hoax—is corroborated by multiple independent fact-checks, including the BBC, Full Fact, PolitiFact, and Vox.

The BBC reported that UK charities including the Samaritans and the UK Safer Internet Centre said there was no verified evidence linking the Momo meme to self-harm, and that police in the UK had not reported any instances of children harming themselves because of it.

Full Fact concluded that the Momo Challenge was an online hoax fuelled by media coverage and a feedback loop in which police statements based on press reports were then cited by the press as further confirmation.

The article correctly identifies the Momo image as a photo of a sculpture by a Japanese special-effects company, which independent reporting confirms is Link Factory artist Keisuke Aiso's silicone piece based on the ubume folklore figure, first shown at Tokyo's Vanilla Gallery in 2016.

The piece's claim that YouTube publicly said it had no evidence of videos promoting the Momo Challenge is supported by YouTube's February 27, 2019 statement, which was also cited by PolitiFact and Full Fact.

The article's anecdote about Kim Kardashian West amplifying a copy-paste Momo warning to her roughly 129 million Instagram followers is independently confirmed by Vox, which described how her post helped push the panic into the US mainstream.

For necessary context that the article only briefly nods to, earlier reports tying Momo to specific child deaths in Argentina, Colombia, Russia, and France were never confirmed by authorities, according to PolitiFact, Full Fact, and Vox.

A genuine, separate problem the article distinguishes from the Momo hoax—disturbing or self-harm content spliced into unofficial children's videos on YouTube such as fake Peppa Pig uploads—is also documented by the BBC and Full Fact.

In 2019, artist Keisuke Aiso told AFP and other outlets that he had already destroyed the deteriorating sculpture and said children could be reassured that 'Momo is dead,' further undercutting the idea of an active Momo threat.

One limitation of the article is that it is a commentary piece relying largely on the author's reporting and observations rather than original data, so readers should pair it with the fact-checks and primary statements above for the strongest evidentiary basis.

Bottom line: the article's core claim that the Momo Challenge is a hoax legitimized by viral sharing and credulous news coverage is well supported by independent fact-checkers, platform statements, and the artist behind the image, making it a trustworthy debunking despite its opinionated tone.
